metadata.caseName: EREsp 1.213.143/RS — IPI Tax Credit Jurisprudential Divergence
metadata.description: Case C: Jurisprudential divergence and unification. The dataset replaces the former synthetic STF panel split with a real STJ divergence over whether Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999 allows IPI credits for taxed inputs used in non-taxed final products. The First Turma had allowed credit; the Second Turma had adopted a restrictive view; the First Section resolved the divergence in EREsp 1.213.143/RS.
metadata.jurisdiction: Brazil — Superior Tribunal de Justiça (STJ), First and Second Public Law Panels and First Section
metadata.demonstration: Shows how multiple concurrent Interpretive Versions can coexist during a jurisprudential split and later be terminated by a unifying adjudication event.
metadata.intervalSemantics: half-open intervals: start <= target < end; null end means current/open-ended

items.label: Law 9.779/1999, Art. 11 — IPI Credit for Desonerated Outputs

metadata.description: Statutory provision allowing use of IPI credit balances arising from taxed inputs applied in industrialization, expressly mentioning exempt and zero-rated products.
Constitutional background provision establishing the non-cumulative principle for IPI.

items.label: AgInt nos EDcl no REsp 1.213.143/RS — First Turma

metadata.description: First Turma decision allowing IPI credit for taxed inputs used in final products that are exempt, non-taxed, or zero-rated.
Second Turma precedent cited in EREsp 1.213.143/RS as adopting a restrictive position against credit for non-taxed final products.
First Section decision resolving the divergence and admitting IPI credit for taxed inputs used in non-taxed final products after Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999.

items.label: Prevailing vote of Minister Regina Helena Costa

metadata.description: Prevailing vote concluding that Law 9.779/1999 authorizes IPI credit for taxed inputs used in non-taxed final products.

textUnits.content: O saldo credor do Imposto sobre Produtos Industrializados - IPI, acumulado em cada trimestre-calendário, decorrente de aquisição de matéria-prima, produto intermediário e material de embalagem, aplicados na industrialização, inclusive de produto isento ou tributado à alíquota zero, que o contribuinte não puder compensar com o IPI devido na saída de outros produtos, poderá ser utilizado de conformidade com o disposto nos arts. 73 e 74 da Lei nº 9.430, de 1996.

Article 11 allows accumulated IPI credit from taxed inputs to be used when the taxpayer cannot offset it against IPI due on other outputs. The litigation concerned whether the wording also covers non-taxed final products, beyond exempt and zero-rated outputs.

Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999 should be interpreted restrictively. The statutory benefit covers products that are exempt or subject to zero rate, but does not extend to final products classified as non-taxed. A broader reading would improperly expand a tax benefit beyond its legal terms.
IPI credit is not available for taxed inputs used in final products whose output is merely non-taxed; the benefit is limited to exempt or zero-rated outputs.

Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999 allows the use of IPI credits from taxed inputs and raw materials when they are applied in the industrialization of final products that are exempt, non-taxed, or subject to zero rate.
IPI credit is allowed for taxed inputs used in final products that are exempt, non-taxed, or zero-rated.

The use of IPI credits for taxed inputs used in non-taxed final products is grounded in the autonomous tax benefit created by Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999, not merely in direct constitutional non-cumulativity. Exempt, zero-rated, and non-taxed outputs are equivalent for the practical purpose of the benefit, and an infralegal act cannot restrict the statutory benefit.
After the enactment of Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999, taxpayers may use IPI credits arising from taxed inputs applied in the industrialization of non-taxed final products.

Article 11 of Law 9.779/1999 created an autonomous legal benefit for the use of IPI credits in desonerated output situations. Since the benefit is not reducible to direct constitutional non-cumulativity, the restrictive administrative exclusion of non-taxed outputs cannot defeat the statutory authorization.

metadata.label: First Section resolution of IPI credit divergence

sampleQueries.interpretation: The query returns |V_valid| = 3: the statutory text plus two conflicting STJ panel interpretations. A reasoning agent should report the split rather than choose a final answer autonomously.
After EREsp 1.213.143/RS, the First Section interpretive version terminates the two panel-level conflicting interpretations and supplies the unified STJ position.
Timeline of actions affecting Article 11: restrictive panel interpretation, expansive panel interpretation, and First Section unification.

notes.replacementOfSyntheticDataset: This file replaces the former synthetic STF panel split with a real STJ divergence resolved by EREsp 1.213.143/RS.
notes.jurisprudentialSplit: The model preserves the statutory version alongside conflicting panel-level interpretive versions during the period of uncertainty.
notes.resolutionMechanisms: The First Section unification action terminates the panel-level conflicting I-TVs and produces a controlling section-level I-TV.
notes.practicalImplications: For compliance analytics, the metadata allows reasoning agents to identify the period of divergence, report uncertainty, and distinguish panel-level precedents from section-level unification.
notes.topologyInsight: The target Item is Law 9.779/1999, Art. 11, rather than only the constitutional non-cumulativity clause, because the STJ's prevailing reasoning treats the credit as an autonomous statutory benefit.
